items.question: What is an Information System?
items.answer: An Information System (IS) is a formal, sociotechnical, organizational system designed to collect, process, store, and distribute information. In a broader sense, it is a combination of hardware, software, and telecommunications networks that people build and use to collect, create, and distribute useful data.

items.title: Data vs. Information
content: Data: Raw facts and figures without context (e.g., a list of numbers).
Information: Data that has been processed, organized, and structured to be meaningful (e.g., a monthly sales report).

items.content: A standard Information System is composed of five major components that interact to produce information:

content: Hardware: The physical devices (servers, workstations, mobile devices).
Software: The programs and applications that instruct the hardware.
Data: The bridge between the machine side and the human side.
Procedures: The rules and instructions for the people using the system.
People: The most important component; those who operate and use the system.

items.title: Transaction Processing Systems (TPS)
items.content: Focuses on the daily, routine transactions necessary to conduct business.
Example: Point-of-Sale (POS) systems or payroll processing.

items.title: Management Information Systems (MIS)
items.content: Provides middle management with reports on the organization's current performance.
Example: Inventory status reports or annual budgets.

items.title: Decision Support Systems (DSS)
items.content: Focuses on problems that are unique and rapidly changing; provides data-driven support for non-routine decisions.

items.question: Who uses DSS?
items.answer: Senior and middle management use these systems to analyze internal and external data for strategic planning.

items.title: The Waterfall Model Phases
content: 1. Planning: Defining the scope and objectives.
4. Implementation: Coding and installing the system.
5. Maintenance: Updating and fixing the system over time.
